title: The Matrix of a Linear Transformation

content.text:
{In the beginning was the Word.}{John 1:1}
 {Mathematics, rightly viewed, possesses not only truth, but supreme beauty.}{— Bertrand Russell}

Every linear transformation from $\R^n$ to $\R^m$ can be represented 
by an $m \times n$ matrix.

content.title: Matrix Representation
content.content:
Let $T: \R^n \to \R^m$ be a linear transformation. Then there exists 
a unique $m \times n$ matrix $A$ such that $T(\vect{x}) = A\vect{x}$ 
for all $\vect{x} \in \R^n$.

The matrix $A$ is called the **standard matrix** 
of $T$, and its columns are $T(\vect{e}_1), T(\vect{e}_2), \ldots, T(\vect{e}_n)$:
\[
A = \begin{bmatrix} T(\vect{e}_1) & T(\vect{e}_2) & \cdots & T(\vect{e}_n) \end{bmatrix}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
For any $\vect{x} = x_1\vect{e}_1 + \cdots + x_n\vect{e}_n$:
\[
T(\vect{x}) = x_1T(\vect{e}_1) + \cdots + x_nT(\vect{e}_n) = A\vect{x}
\]
where $A$ has columns $T(\vect{e}_i)$.

content.title: Finding the standard matrix
content.problem:
Find the standard matrix of $T: \R^2 \to \R^3$ defined by 
$T(x, y) = (x + 2y, 3x - y, x)$.

content.text:
For linear transformations between general finite-dimensional vector 
spaces, we need to choose bases.

content.title: Matrix Relative to Bases
content.content:
Let $T: V \to W$ be linear, $\mathcal{B}$ a basis for $V$, and 
$\mathcal{C}$ a basis for $W$. The **matrix of $T$ relative to 
$\mathcal{B}$ and $\mathcal{C}$**, denoted $[T]_{\mathcal{C} \leftarrow \mathcal{B}}$, 
is the matrix whose $j$-th column is $[T(\vect{b}_j)]_\mathcal{C}$.

marginNotes.text: In him all things hold together.
